[[sn:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q28608917]] Wikidata name: Tenno-no-mori Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|[[:en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|Tenno-no-Mori Kofun]]|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa

Guide|publisher=[[Kodansha]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)}}}} - Link to Asahi Shimbun "Kotobank." == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} * {{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] - Kyoto City Information Center {{translated page|ja|天皇の杜古墳|version=104551982|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[Category:Otokuni Kofun Group]] Kofun]] {{wikidata link|Q28608917}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q28608917}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT [[sn:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q28608917]] Wikidata name: Tenno-no-mori Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|[[:en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|Tenno-no-Mori Kofun]]|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Research Center|isbn=|pages=45-46|ref={{Harvid|Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei]] 27 Kyoto City|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490271|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=Haruo Wada|year=1989|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Kofun Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Tokyo-do Shuppan]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=|chapter=[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87%E3%81%AE%E6%9D%9C%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-1444522 Tenno-no-mori Kofun]|title=National Historic Site Guide|publisher=[[Kodansha]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)}}}} - Link to Asahi Shimbun "Kotobank." == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} * {{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] - Kyoto City Information Center {{translated page|ja|天皇の杜古墳|version=104551982|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[Category:Otokuni Kofun Group]] "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa

Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Research Center|isbn=|pages=45-46|ref={{Harvid|Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=Haruo Wada|year=1989|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Kofun Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Tokyo-do Shuppan]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} ''' {{Cite [[sn:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q28608917]] Wikidata name: Tenno-no-mori Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|[[:en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|Tenno-no-Mori Kofun]]|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural revision.text:
[[sn:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q28608917]] Wikidata name: Tenno-no-mori Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|[[:en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|Tenno-no-Mori Kofun]]|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Research Center|isbn=|pages=45-46|ref={{Harvid|Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei]] 27 Kyoto City|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490271|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=Haruo Wada|year=1989|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Kofun Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Tokyo-do Shuppan]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=|chapter=[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87%E3%81%AE%E6%9D%9C%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-1444522 Tenno-no-mori Kofun]|title=National Historic Site Guide|publisher=[[Kodansha]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)}}}} - Link to Asahi Shimbun "Kotobank." == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} * {{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] - Kyoto City Information Center {{translated page|ja|天皇の杜古墳|version=104551982|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[Category:Otokuni Kofun Group]] [[Category:National historic sites in Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun of Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:History of Nishikyo-ku, Kyoto]] {{Commons category|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} [[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q28608917}}

Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Research Center|isbn=|pages=45-46|ref={{Harvid|Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei]] 27 Kyoto City|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490271|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=Haruo Wada|year=1989|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Kofun Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Tokyo-do Shuppan]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} ''' {{Cite Guide|publisher=[[Kodansha]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)}}}} - Link to Asahi Shimbun "Kotobank." == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} * {{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] - Kyoto City Information Center {{translated page|ja|天皇の杜古墳|version=104551982|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] }} [[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[Category:Otokuni Kofun Group]] It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa

Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site [[commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[sn:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q28608917]] Wikidata name: Tenno-no-mori Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|[[:en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|Tenno-no-Mori Kofun]]|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa

[[co:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[sn:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q28608917]] Wikidata name: Tenno-no-mori Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|[[:en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|Tenno-no-Mori Kofun]]|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Research Center|isbn=|pages=45-46|ref={{Harvid|Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei]] 27 Kyoto City|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490271|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=Haruo Wada|year=1989|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Kofun Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Tokyo-do Shuppan]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=|chapter=[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87%E3%81%AE%E6%9D%9C%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-1444522 Tenno-no-mori Kofun]|title=National Historic Site Guide|publisher=[[Kodansha]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)}}}} - Link to Asahi Shimbun "Kotobank." == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} * {{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] - Kyoto City Information Center {{translated page|ja|天皇の杜古墳|version=104551982|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Commons category|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} [[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]] {{wikidata link|Q28608917}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:History of Nishikyo-ku, Kyoto]] [[Category:Kofun of Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:National historic sites in Kyoto prefecture]] [[Category:Otokuni Kofun Group]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Zenpokoenfun]]

Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa

The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Research Center|isbn=|pages=45-46|ref={{Harvid|Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=Haruo Wada|year=1989|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Kofun Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Tokyo-do Shuppan]]|isbn=4490102607|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Kofun)|1989}}}} ''' {{Cite Guide|publisher=[[Kodansha]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)}}}} - Link to Asahi Shimbun "Kotobank." == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} * [[co:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[sn:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpokoenfun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q28608917]] Wikidata name: Tenno-no-mori Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:30, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|[[:en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|Tenno-no-Mori Kofun]]|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National [[:en:Historic site|Historic site of Japan]]} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with [[:en:Emperor Montoku|Emperor Montoku]], the 55th Emperor of Japan from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between [[:en:1988|1988]] and [[:en:1989|1989]].Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include [[:en:Fukiishi|fukiishi]] and rows of [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late [[:en:4th century|4th century]] during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in [[:en:1922|1922]], and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as [[:en:Igenoyama Kofun|Igenoyama Kofun]] and [[:en:Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun]] into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the [[:en:Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum]].}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71]}}|publisher=Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Research Center|isbn=|pages=45-46|ref={{Harvid|Kyoto Prefectural Archaeological Information, Issue 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=|chapter=[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A9%E7%9A%87%E3%81%AE%E6%9D%9C%E5%8F%A4%E5%A2%B3-1444522 Tenno-no-mori Kofun]|title=National Historic Site Guide|publisher=[[Kodansha]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)}}}} - Link to Asahi Shimbun "Kotobank." == External Links == {{Commonscat|Tenno-no-mori Kofun}} *
