[[co:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[co:commons:Category:Tenno-no-mori Kofun]][[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] [[en:Tennō-no-Mori Kofun]][[ja:天皇の杜古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|image=File:Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenkei.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.977511|N|135.68965|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architectural_styles={{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|WD=Q11268718|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|2=ja|3=前方後円墳|4=ko|5=전방후원분|6=zh|7=前方後圓墳}}|part_of={{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|WD=Q123117022|1=Otokuni Kofun Cluster}}}} {{nihongo|{{ill|Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|en|Tennō-no-Mori Kofun|WD=Q28608917|lt=Tenno-no-Mori Kofun|1=Tenno-no-Mori Kofun}}|[[:ja:天皇の杜古墳|天皇の杜古墳]]}} is a {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}} located in {{ill|Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja|京都市西京区御陵塚ノ越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misasagi Tsukanokoshi-chō, Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Kyoto Prefecture. It is designated as a National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan|1=Historic site}}} (as part of the {{ill|Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja|乙訓古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otokuni Kofun Cluster|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491395|1=Otokuni Kofun Cluster}}). == Overview == This tumulus was constructed along the western edge of the {{ill|Kyoto Basin|ja|京都盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Basin|1=Kyoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10883263}}, near the foothills on the right bank of the {{ill|Katsura River (Yodo River system)|ja|桂川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsura River (Yodo River system)|1=Katsura River (Yodo River system)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2908720}}. Since the medieval period, the mound has been traditionally associated with {{ill|Emperor Montoku|en|Emperor Montoku|WD=Q357225|lt=Emperor Montoku|1=Emperor Montoku}}, the 55th Emperor of Japan from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. The name "Tenno-no-mori" (lit. "Emperor's Forest") and the surrounding area name "Misasagi" (imperial mausoleum) are said to derive from this tradition. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} The mound remains well-preserved, and excavations were conducted between {{ill|1988|en|1988|WD=Q2426|lt=1988|1=1988}} and {{ill|1989|en|1989|WD=Q2425|lt=1989|1=1989}}.Site explanation board. The tumulus has a distinctive "mirror-handle" keyhole shape with an unexpanded front portion facing south-southeast. The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi|1=Fukiishi}} and rows of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century|WD=Q8099|lt=4th century|1=4th century}} during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270|1=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in {{ill|1922|en|1922|WD=Q2165|lt=1922|1=1922}}, and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as {{ill|Igenoyama Kofun|en|Igenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11492427|lt=Igenoyama Kofun|1=Igenoyama Kofun}} and {{ill|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11457885|lt=Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Terado Ōtsuka Kofun}} into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the {{ill|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|en|Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|WD=Q11375124|lt=Kyoto City Archaeological Museum|1=Kyoto City Archaeological Museum}}.}} == Cultural Properties == === National Historic Site === * '''Tenno-no-mori Kofun''' (as part of the Otokuni Kofun Cluster) **''' Designated as a National Historic Site on March 8, 1922, with an area of 10,234 square meters. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} **''' Integrated with other tumuli in 2016 to form the "Otokuni Kofun Cluster" designation. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Site explanation board (Kyoto City Cultural Property Protection Section) * Publications by local governments ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1994|chapter=|title=Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|publisher=Kyoto City|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1999|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title={{PDFlink|[http://www.kyotofu-maibun.or.jp/data/kankou/kankou-pdf/jyouhou/kyoutofu-J71.pdf 71|1999}}}} * Other references ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1979|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei]] 27 Kyoto City|publisher=[[Heibonsha]]|isbn=4582490271|ref={{Harvid|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1979}}}} ''' {{Cite book|editor=|author=Haruo Wada|year=1989|chapter=Tenno-no-mori Kofun|title=[[Nihon Kofun The mound is constructed in two tiers. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} Outer surface features include {{ill|Fukiishi|en|Fukiishi|WD=Q11621213|lt=fukiishi|1=Fukiishi}} and rows of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} (cylindrical and morning glory-shaped). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} There is no surrounding moat, but remnants of a demarcated area resembling a moat have been identified. The burial facility has not been investigated. The tumulus is estimated to have been built in the late {{ill|4th century|en|4th century|WD=Q8099|lt=4th century|1=4th century}} during the early {{ill|Middle Kofun period|ja|古墳時代中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Kofun period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Sfn|Tenno-no-mori Kofun (National Historic Site)|ja|天皇の杜古墳（国指定史跡）}} Archaeological evidence suggests the individual buried here was a prominent figure who controlled the Katsura River's right bank. During this period, the transition from keyhole-shaped to circular mounds is evident in the {{ill|Mukō Hills|ja|向日丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukō Hills|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} tombs such as {{ill|Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja|妙見山古墳 (向日市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōkenyama Kofun (Mukō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579270|1=Q135579270}} and {{ill|Den-Takabatake Tomb|ja|伝高畠陵古墳|1=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|lt=Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatake Tomb|13=User:Immanuelle/Den-Takabatakeryo Kofun|qq=}}. However, this site stands out as a large keyhole-shaped tumulus, indicating political importance surpassing the Mukō Hills. The site was designated as a National Historic Site in {{ill|1922|en|1922|WD=Q2165|lt=1922|1=1922}}, and it has been developed into a public historical park. == Site History == * '''1922 (Taishō 11)''': Designated as a National Historic Site.{{National Historic Site Database|401|1638|Otokuni Kofun Cluster}} * '''1981-1982 (Shōwa 56-57)''': Public ownership of the surrounding area was established. {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * '''1988-1989 (Shōwa 63-Heisei 1)''': Excavations were conducted. * '''1994 (Heisei 6)''': Opening of the historical park. * '''2016 (Heisei 28)''': The National Historic Site "Tenno-no-mori Kofun" was integrated with other tumuli such as {{ill|Igenoyama Kofun|en|Igenoyama Kofun|WD=Q11492427|lt=Igenoyama Kofun|1=Igenoyama Kofun}} and {{ill|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|en|Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|WD=Q11457885|lt=Terado Ōtsuka Kofun|1=Terado Ōtsuka Kofun}} into the expanded designation "Otokuni Kofun Cluster." Eight additional tumuli were included in this designation.March 1, 2016, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology Notification No. 31. [http://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/2015112003.html "Regarding Designation of Historic Sites, etc."] (Agency for Cultural Affairs, press release, November 20, 2015). == Mound Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows:[http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005646.html Tenno-no-mori Kofun] (Kyoto City Information Center). {{Sfn|Report on Preservation and Maintenance of Tenno-no-mori Kofun|1994|pp=5-11}} * Total length: 83.0 meters * Posterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 50.5 meters ''' Height: 7.2 meters * Anterior section (two-tiered): ''' Width: 33.5 meters ''' Height: 4.8 meters Tenno-no-mori Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|Posterior mound top Tenno-no-mori Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View from posterior to anterior mound 天皇の杜古墳出土 円筒埴輪 (京都市考古資料館展示).JPG|Cylindrical haniwa
